Project Overview¶

Ecommerce Marketing Analysis¶

This project analyzes customer behavior for an ecommerce company using SQL, Python, data visualization, and machine learning.

The goal is to identify:

  • Factors that drive Customer Lifetime Value (LTV)
  • Factors associated with customer churn
  • Customer segments based on purchasing behavior and risk
  • Opportunities to improve retention and revenue

The project combines exploratory data analysis, correlation analysis, predictive modeli

Importing Libraries and dataset¶

In [70]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import (
    accuracy_score,
    classification_report,
    confusion_matrix,
    mean_absolute_error,
    mean_squared_error,
    r2_score
)
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
import os

In [4]:
def dataset_overview(df):
    print("Dataset Overview")
    print ("="*50)
    print(f"Shape: {df.shape[0]} rows, {df.shape[1]} columns\n")
    
    print(" Column Infomation")
    print("="*50)
    info = pd.DataFrame({
        "Data Type": df.dtypes,
        "Missing Values": df.isnull().sum(),
        "Missing %": round((df.isnull().sum() / len(df)) * 100, 2)
    })
    print(info)
    print("\n")
    
    print(" Descriptive Statistics (Numerical columns)")
    print("="*50)
    display(df.describe().T)
    print("\n")
    
    print(" Unique Value counts")
    print("="*50)
    unique_counts = df.nunique().sort_values(ascending=False)
    print(unique_counts.head(15))
    print("\n")
    
    print(" First 5 Rows")
    print("="*50)
    display(df.head())

Duplicate Check¶

In [6]:
def check_duplicates(df, subset=None, drop=False, preview_rows=10):
    
    # Normalize subset
    if subset is not None and isinstance(subset, str):
        subset = [subset]

    # Column validation
    if subset is not None:
        missing = [c for c in subset if c not in df.columns]
        if missing:
            raise ValueError(f"Columns not found in DataFrame: {missing}")

    # Count duplicates
    dup_mask = df.duplicated(subset=subset)
    dup_count = int(dup_mask.sum())

    label = "all columns" if subset is None else subset
    print(f" Duplicate rows based on {label}: {dup_count}")

    # Preview all members of duplicate groups
    if dup_count > 0:
        preview = df[df.duplicated(subset=subset, keep=False)].head(preview_rows)
        display(preview)
    else:
        print(" No duplicates found.")

    # Optionally drop
    if drop and dup_count > 0:
        before = df.shape
        df.drop_duplicates(subset=subset, inplace=True)
        after = df.shape
        print(f" Duplicates removed. Shape {before} ➜ {after}")

    return df, dup_count

Outlier Check¶

In [9]:
 def detect_outliers(df, method="iqr", factor=1.5, cols=None, 
                        return_outliers=False, show_plots=False, n_cols_plot=3):

        if cols is None:
            cols = df.select_dtypes(include="number").columns
            
        summary = []
        outlier_index = set()
        
        for col in cols:
            s = df[col].dropna()
            if s.empty:
                continue
                
            if method == "iqr":
                q1, q3 = s.quantile([0.25, 0.75])
                iqr = q3 - q1
                low, high = q1 - factor * iqr, q3 + factor * iqr
                mask = (df[col] < low) | (df[col] > high)
            elif metod == "z":
                mean, std = s.mean(), s.std()
                z_scores = (df[col] - mean) / std
                mask = (z_scores.abs() > factor)
                low, high = mean - factor * std, mean + factor * std
            else:
                raise ValueError("Method must be either 'iqr' or 'z'")
                
            count = mask.sum()
            pct = round(count / len(df) * 100, 2)
            summary.append({
                "column": col,
                "low_bound": round(low, 2),
                "high_bound": round(high, 2),
                "outlier_count": count,
                "outlier_%": pct
            })
            
            outlier_index.update(df[mask].index)
            
        summary_df = pd.DataFrame(summary).sort_values("outlier_%", ascending=False)
        
        if show_plots:
            num_plots = len(cols)
            rows = (num_plots + n_cols_plot - 1) // n_cols_plot
            plt.figure(figsize=(6 * n_cols_plot, 4 * rows))
            for i, col in enumerate(cols, 1):
                plt.subplot(rows, n_cols_plot, i)
                sns.boxplot(x=df[col], color='skyblue')
                plt.title(f"{col}\nOutliers: {summary_df.loc[summary_df['column']==col, 'outlier_%'].values[0]}%")
            plt.tight_layout()
            plt.show()
        
        if return_outliers:
            outlier_rows = df.loc[outlier_index].copy()
            return summary_df, outlier_rows
        else:
            return summary_df
In [10]:
detect_outliers(df, show_plots=True)

Demographic Overview¶

In [11]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))

plt.hist(df['age'], bins=10)

plt.title('Customer Age Distribution')
plt.xlabel('Age')
plt.ylabel('Number of Customers')

plt.show()

In [14]:
city_counts = df['city'].value_counts().head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))

city_counts.plot(kind='bar')

plt.title('Top 10 Cities by Customer Count')
plt.xlabel('City')
plt.ylabel('Number of Customers')

plt.show()

Demographic Analysis Summary¶

  • Most customers were between 25 and 50 years old, with the highest concentration in the mid-to-late 30s.
  • Gender distribution was relatively balanced.
  • The United States represented the largest customer market.
  • Major cities such as Houston, Phoenix, New York, Chicago, and Los Angeles contained the largest customer populations.

Correlation - Lifetime Value¶

In [15]:
corr_ltv = (
    df.corr(numeric_only=True)['lifetime_value']
      .sort_values(ascending=False)
      .round(2)
)

print(corr_ltv)

corr_ltv = corr.drop('lifetime_value')

top_corr = pd.concat([
    corr_ltv.head(10),
    corr_ltv.tail(2)
])

plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
top_corr.sort_values().plot(kind='barh')

plt.title('Top Factors Associated with Customer Lifetime Value')
plt.xlabel('Correlation Coefficient')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Correlation Analysis¶

Lifetime Value

  • Total purchases showed the strongest positive relationship with lifetime value.
  • Login frequency, mobile app usage, and social media engagement were positively related to customer value.
  • Cart abandonment rate showed a strong negative relationship with lifetime value.

Churn

  • Cart abandonment rate and customer service calls showed positive relationships with churn.
  • Email engagement, mobile app usage, and purchasing activity were negatively related to churn.
  • Engagement metrics generally indicated lower churn risk.

Login Frequency vs Lifetime Value¶

In [21]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))

sns.regplot(
    data=df,
    x='login_frequency',
    y='lifetime_value',
    scatter_kws={'alpha':0.2}
)

plt.title('Login Frequency vs Lifetime Value')
plt.xlabel('Login Frequency')
plt.ylabel('Lifetime Value')

plt.show()

    data=df,
    x='social_media_engagement_score',
    y='lifetime_value',
    scatter_kws={'alpha':0.2}
)

plt.title('Social Media Engagement vs Lifetime Value')
plt.xlabel('Social Media Engagement')
plt.ylabel('Lifetime Value')

plt.show()

sns.regplot(
    data=df,
    x='mobile_app_usage',
    y='lifetime_value',
    scatter_kws={'alpha':0.2}
)

plt.title('Mobile App Usage vs Lifetime Value')
plt.xlabel('Mobile App Usage')
plt.ylabel('Lifetime Value')

plt.show()

Cart Abandonment vs Lifetime Value¶

In [24]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))

sns.regplot(
    data=df,
    x='cart_abandonment_rate',
    y='lifetime_value',
    scatter_kws={'alpha':0.2}
)

plt.title('Cart Abandonment vs Lifetime Value')
plt.xlabel('Cart Abandonment')
plt.ylabel('Lifetime Value')

plt.show()

Customer Lifetime Value¶

  • Customers with higher login frequency tended to generate greater lifetime value.
  • Social media engagement showed a positive relationship with customer value.
  • Mobile app usage was associated with higher lifetime value.
  • Higher cart abandonment rates were associated with lower customer value.

Customer Service Calls vs Churned¶

In [25]:
churn_calls = df.groupby('churned')['customer_service_calls'].mean()

churn_calls.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('Average Customer Service Calls by Churn Status')
plt.xlabel('Churned')
plt.ylabel('Average Service Calls')
plt.show()

Email Engagement vs Churned¶

In [26]:
churn_calls = df.groupby('churned')['email_open_rate'].mean()

churn_calls.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('Average Email Open Rate by Churn Status')
plt.xlabel('Churned')
plt.ylabel('Average Email Open Rate')
plt.show()

Customer Churn¶

  • Churned customers made more customer service calls on average.
  • Active customers exhibited higher email engagement than churned customers.
  • Engagement-related metrics generally aligned with lower churn rates.

Churn Prediction Model¶

In [27]:
## predictor variabled selected based on analysis
features = [
    'session_duration_avg',
    'pages_per_session',
    'wishlist_items',
    'days_since_last_purchase',
    'returns_rate',
    'email_open_rate',
    'product_reviews_written',
    'social_media_engagement_score',
    'mobile_app_usage',
    'payment_method_diversity',
    'credit_balance',
    'membership_years',
    'login_frequency',
    'cart_abandonment_rate',
    'total_purchases',
    'average_order_value',
    'customer_service_calls'
]

## defining x and y
x = df[features]
y = df['churned']

## Train and Test split
x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    x,
    y,
    test_size=0.2,
    random_state=42,
    stratify=y
)

## Training the model
model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=5000)

model.fit(x_train, y_train)

## Predictions
y_pred = model.predict(x_test)

## Probabilities
y_prob = model.predict_proba(x_test)[:,1]

## Evaluation
print("Accuracy:", accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred))
print("\nClassification Report")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred))

print("\nConfusion Matrix")
print(confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred))

## Add probabilities back to dataframe
df['churn_probability'] = model.predict_proba(x)[:,1]

## Risk Levels
df['risk_level'] = pd.cut(
    df['churn_probability'],
    bins=[0, 0.3, 0.7, 1],
    labels=['Low Risk', 'Medium Risk', 'High Risk']
)

print("\nRisk Level Distribution")
print(df['risk_level'].value_counts())

Accuracy: 0.7679143314651722

Classification Report

Summary:¶

A Logistic Regression model achieved an accuracy of 76.8%. The model performed well in identifying retained customers (94% recall) but was less effective at detecting churned customers (34% recall). This suggests that customer churn behavior may be influenced by more complex relationships than can be captured through a linear model alone. Therefore, more advanced machine learning techniques such as Random Forests will be explored in subsequent analysis.

Churn Prediction Model: Random forest¶

Creating the model¶

In [31]:
# Create Model
rf_model = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=200,
    max_depth=10,
    random_state=42
)


# Train
rf_model.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Predictions
rf_pred = rf_model.predict(x_test)

# Probabilities
rf_prob = rf_model.predict_proba(x_test)[:,1]

# Evaluation
print("Accuracy:", accuracy_score(y_test, rf_pred))

print("\nClassification Report")
print(classification_report(y_test, rf_pred))

print("\nConfusion Matrix")
print(confusion_matrix(y_test, rf_pred))

Accuracy: 0.8604883907125701

Classification Report
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.86      0.96      0.91      7103
           1       0.87      0.61      0.72      2889

    accuracy                           0.86      9992
   macro avg       0.86      0.79      0.81      9992
weighted avg       0.86      0.86      0.85      9992


Confusion Matrix
[[6840  263]
 [1131 1758]]

    df['rf_churn_probability'],
    bins=[0,0.3,0.7,1],

rf_risk_level
Low Risk       34438

Name: count, dtype: int64

Random Forest Churn Prediction Model¶

  • Random Forest outperformed Logistic Regression, achieving approximately 85% accuracy.
  • Cart abandonment rate was the strongest predictor of churn.
  • Customer service calls, total purchases, average order value, and days since last purchase were also important factors.
  • Churn probabilities were used to classify customers into risk levels.

Random Forest Lifetime Value Prediction Model¶

Create Model¶

In [45]:
# Features
features = [
    'session_duration_avg',
    'pages_per_session',
    'wishlist_items',
    'days_since_last_purchase',
    'returns_rate',
    'email_open_rate',
    'product_reviews_written',
    'social_media_engagement_score',
    'mobile_app_usage',
    'payment_method_diversity',
    'credit_balance',
    'membership_years',
    'login_frequency',
    'cart_abandonment_rate',
    'total_purchases',
    'average_order_value',
    'customer_service_calls'
]

X = df[features]
y = df['lifetime_value']

# Split
x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    x,
    y,
    test_size=0.2,
    random_state=42
)

# Model
rf_ltv = RandomForestRegressor(
    n_estimators=200,
    max_depth=10,
    random_state=42
)

# Train
rf_ltv.fit(x_train, y_train)

# Predict
y_pred = rf_ltv.predict(x_test)

# Metrics
print("R2 Score:", r2_score(y_test, y_pred))
print("MAE:", mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_pred))
print("RMSE:", mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred) ** 0.5)

# Add predictions back to dataframe
df['predicted_lifetime_value'] = rf_ltv.predict(X)

R2 Score: 0.9310649443508398
MAE: 162.33767788509832
RMSE: 243.37016860321646

Prepare data for clustering model¶

In [53]:
df['predicted_lifetime_value'] = rf_ltv.predict(x)
df['rf_churn_probability'] = rf_model.predict_proba(x)[:,1]
In [54]:

    'rf_churn_probability']].head()
Out[54]:

Random Forest Lifetime Value Prediction Model¶

  • The model achieved an R² score of approximately 0.93.
  • Total purchases and average order value were the strongest drivers of customer value.
  • Engagement metrics contributed to customer value but had less influence than purchasing behavior.
  • Results aligned closely with findings from the SQL and correlation analyses.

K-Means Clustering Model¶

In [58]:
# Select features for clustering
cluster_features = [
    'predicted_lifetime_value',
    'rf_churn_probability',
    'total_purchases',
    'average_order_value',
    'cart_abandonment_rate'
]

# Scale data
x_cluster = df[cluster_features]

scaler = StandardScaler()

x_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(x_cluster)

# Elbow Method
inertia = []

for k in range(1, 11):
    kmeans = KMeans(
        n_clusters=k,
        random_state=42,
        n_init=10
    )

    kmeans.fit(x_scaled)

    inertia.append(kmeans.inertia_)

# Plot Elbow Curve
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))

plt.plot(
    range(1,11),
    inertia,
    marker='o'
)

plt.title('Elbow Method for Optimal Number of Customer Segments')
plt.xlabel('Number of Clusters')
plt.ylabel('Inertia')

plt.grid(True)

plt.show()

Creating the segments¶

In [59]:
# Create K-Means model
kmeans = KMeans(
    n_clusters=4,
    random_state=42,
    n_init=10
)

# Fit and assign clsuters
df['customer_segment'] = kmeans.fit_predict(x_scaled)

# Check segment sizes
print(df['customer_segment'].value_counts().sort_index())

customer_segment
0    13365
1     9478
2    27092
3       25
Name: count, dtype: int64

Visualize the clusters¶

In [64]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

for segment in sorted(df['customer_segment'].unique()):
    segment_data = df[df['customer_segment'] == segment]
    
    plt.scatter(
        segment_data['predicted_lifetime_value'],
        segment_data['rf_churn_probability'],
        label=f'Segment {segment}',
        alpha=0.5
    )
    
plt.title('Customer Segments by Predicted Lifetime Value and Churn Risk')
plt.xlabel('Predicted Lifetime Value')
plt.ylabel('Churn Probability')
plt.legend(title='Customer Segment')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Analyze customer segments¶

In [60]:
segment_profile = df.groupby('customer_segment')[[
    'predicted_lifetime_value',
    'rf_churn_probability',
    'total_purchases',
    'average_order_value',
    'cart_abandonment_rate'
]].mean().round(2)

print(segment_profile)

                  predicted_lifetime_value  rf_churn_probability  \
customer_segment                                                   
0                                  2457.24                  0.25   
1                                   871.86                  0.67   
2                                  1136.46                  0.18   
3                                  2105.98                  0.44   

                  total_purchases  average_order_value  cart_abandonment_rate  
customer_segment                                                               
0                           20.93               135.24                  40.26  
1                            7.16               126.26                  76.15  
2                           11.36               109.13                  58.72  
3                           14.40              7593.42                  54.66

Naming the clusters¶

In [67]:
segment_mapping = {
    0: 'VIP Customers',
    1: 'At-Risk Customers',
    2: 'Core Customer Base',
    3: 'High-Spend Outliers'
}

df['segment_name'] = df['customer_segment'].map(segment_mapping)

K-Means Customer Segmentation¶

  • Four customer segments were identified using lifetime value, churn probability, purchasing behavior, and engagement metrics.
  • High-value customers exhibited stronger purchasing and engagement behavior.
  • Lower-value segments showed higher churn risk and cart abandonment rates.
  • Customer segments will be incorporated into the Tableau dashboard to compare value, risk, and customer behavior across groups.

Key Insights¶

Customer Engagement Drives Customer Value¶

  • Customers with higher login frequency, mobile app usage, email engagement, and social media engagement consistently generated higher lifetime value.
  • Correlation analysis and visualizations supported the findings from the SQL analysis.
  • Customers who interacted with the platform more frequently tended to become more valuable over time.

Cart Abandonment Is a Strong Indicator of Customer Risk¶

  • Customers with higher cart abandonment rates generated significantly lower lifetime value.
  • Cart abandonment showed one of the strongest relationships with both churn and customer value.
  • The churn prediction model identified cart abandonment rate as the most important factor influencing churn risk.

Customer Service Calls May Signal Customer Dissatisfaction¶

  • Customers who contacted customer service more frequently tended to have lower lifetime value.
  • Customer service calls were one of the strongest predictors of churn in the machine learning model.
  • Increased support activity may indicate unresolved issues that could lead to customer loss.

Purchasing Behavior Is the Primary Driver of Lifetime Value¶

  • Customers who made more purchases and spent more per order consistently generated greater lifetime value.
  • Total purchases and average order value were the most important variables in the lifetime value prediction model.
  • Purchase frequency and spending habits were the strongest indicators of long-term customer value.

Behavioral Metrics Provided More Insight Than Demographics¶

  • Age, gender, and geographic location showed relatively limited impact on customer value and churn.
  • Engagement and purchasing behavior consistently emerged as stronger indicators across the SQL, correlation, and machine learning analyses.
  • Customer behavior was more useful than demographic characteristics when explaining customer outcomes.

Distinct Customer Segments Exist Within the Customer Base¶

  • K-Means clustering identified four customer segments with different levels of value and risk.
  • High-value customers exhibited stronger purchasing and engagement behavior.
  • Lower-value segments showed higher churn probabilities and less favorable customer behaviors.

Combining Multiple Analyses Created a More Complete Customer View¶

  • SQL analysis identified key customer behavior patterns.
  • Correlation analysis validated relationships between variables.
  • Machine learning models predicted churn risk and customer value.
  • Customer segmentation grouped customers into actionable segments for dashboard reporting and business decision-making.
In [69]:
df.to_csv(
    'ecommerce_tableau.csv',
    index=False
)
In [ ]:
